concept: Rule / Stated Normative Requirement, Permission, or Prohibition
description: A stated normative prescription that presents an action, omission, status, permission, or prohibition as applicable under a claimed source, scope, condition, and consequence. A rule may be written or unwritten, formal or informal, followed or ignored, legitimate or illegitimate. The word does not mean a source-specific law, policy, custom, technical specification, method, regular pattern, fact, moral truth, compliance, or enforcement.
sound_symbolism: Necessity 'na' enters the defined contour of 'sholu', giving a norm a stated shape rather than disguising it as natural fact. Three syllables keep the common governance tool easy to name but not self-justifying.
grammatical_notes: A countable noun: 'ha nasholu ha phoa phelona.' (This rule requires this action.) Preserve exact wording, identifiers, versions, and source categories in a separate source record while the Phi account identifies their rule role. Distinct from 'na' (necessity), 'kirosholu' (technical specification), 'norae' (method), custom, law, legitimacy, and 'nashakelu' (enforce).

pillars.solarpunk-values: Shared systems need explicit rules whose sources, burdens, ecological effects, and revision paths can be inspected rather than left as invisible custom.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Naming a rule as a claim about conduct prevents speakers from presenting institutional demands as facts of nature or proof of moral correctness.

tags.cognition: a stated normative prescription with conditions and scope
tags.community: a rule claimed to apply within a social process
